text: I usually start my day with some toast with butter and jam, maybe some tea. If I'm feeling more energetic, I might whip up some scrambled eggs with vegetables from our little garden out back. Nothing fancy, just something simple and comforting to get me going before heading out into whatever the day brings.

I usually make myself a simple sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner, perhaps some soup or a salad if I'm feeling fancy. Sometimes my grandkids will come round and we'll have a picnic together while watching a film or playing games. Other weeks, I might meet some colleagues or friends at our local café for a bite. Nothing too elaborate, really – just something nourishing to hold me going until the afternoon slump kicks in!
I'm a bit of an early bird, I tend to be in bed by 10 o'clock most nights. It helps me feel refreshed and ready to tackle the morning, which is essential since I like getting started early.

I think a cuppa is essential, isn't it? A strong brew gets me going in the morning. And, of course, getting outside into my garden first thing helps set a gentle pace for the day. There's something soothing about being among nature, watching those plants grow... it's meditative, really. As for exercise, I try to squeeze in some brisk walkies – just around the block should be enough to keep the old bones limber. Nothing too strenuous, mind! After all, I'm 70 now, so need to listen to what my body tells me.

I try to be a bit of an informal connector really – you know, making connections between people who might otherwise miss each other. In my former role, we had regular team meetings where everyone could catch up with colleagues outside of work. We'd have little chats before or after those meetings, sharing personal stuff like holidays or weekend plans. It helped build camaraderie, made our jobs more enjoyable. Even now, though I'm retired, I still make sure to reach out to former colleagues and acquaintances whenever I run into them. And when attending local events or joining groups related to my hobbies, I meet new people who share similar interests. Just keeping conversations going and being genuinely interested in others seems key to building genuine relationships, even in professional circles.

Well, since I'm a morning person, I find getting up bright and early helps me feel more focused before starting my workday. Usually, I'll have a nice cuppa tea and sit down at my desk to go through any urgent emails or messages while enjoying some gentle exercise like stretching or yoga. After that, I'll spend a bit of time tidying up my workspace – it really does make a difference having everything organised! Then, I try to tackle the most important task first thing, so I can get out of the way what might be challenging later in the day. Of course, there are distractions at home too, like the telly or my grandkids calling, but I set aside specific breaks throughout the day to catch up with them and refuel. A little treat afterwards always gives me the boost to carry on! It's all quite simple, but I think consistency is key when staying productive from home.

I must admit, I'm still getting used to them, even though my grandsons have been encouraging me to make more use of them. To be honest, I find some aspects quite tricky, like setting everything up properly and dealing with all those technical issues. But once we're actually speaking, I think it helps us catch up so much better than a letter or phone call could. There's something about seeing each other's faces and being able to pick up on expressions that makes our conversations feel more meaningful. Of course, there's always someone to lend a hand – my grandson is very helpful! We usually manage okay, but sometimes he has to guide me through any complicated parts. It's lovely having him near, really.

You know, people say retirement is a time for relaxation, and indeed some parts of it have been lovely so far. But staying motivated has its own set of challenges! For me, setting routines helps quite a bit. Even though my workday ended ages ago, getting out into the garden first thing in the morning gives me something productive to focus on – it clears my mind, gets the fresh air... Just tidying up, pruning, planting seeds – there's satisfaction in seeing everything come together again. Plus, it sets a nice tone for the rest of the day. And writing down what I need to tackle later on, even if only in a notebook, makes sure I'm making progress rather than feeling like I'm drifting aimlessly. Of course, having someone to chat with occasionally always lends motivation too – whether it's my son coming round for tea or meeting a friend at the local café. A spot of exercise usually does wonders for me during those mid-day dips when my energy starts waning either. Overall, finding simple pleasures and keeping myself occupied seems key – perhaps that's why all these little rituals feel more significant now than they used to when I was still working full-time!

I think changes in priorities can be quite challenging, but I'm a bit of an organised soul, so once I have everything laid out before me, I find it easier to adjust to what needs to shift. It really comes down to being flexible, prioritising effectively and communicating clearly with those involved. In my previous roles, we'd usually discuss any upcoming projects or deadlines together as a team, making sure everyone was aware of the revised focus areas. Sometimes, our head might give us guidance on how to reallocate resources, which helps us adapt to the changed circumstances. Of course, some adjustments take more time than others – perhaps due to dependencies or interdependencies – but ultimately, getting the job done is always top priority.

Well, I think I'd always been quite straightforward and assertive in my previous jobs, actually. As a mid-level admin assistant, I was used to speaking clearly and directly with colleagues, heads of departments, and even the odd manager. If something wasn't done correctly or needed fixing, I wouldn't hesitate to mention it politely but firmly. Of course, being part of the education sector meant there were plenty of opportunities to speak up, like raising concerns about resources or staffing issues affecting students. I suppose one key thing is that I value clarity and accuracy above all else – whether it's making sure records are spot-on or explaining complex information simply. When asking for changes or improvements, I try breaking them down into manageable steps so everyone involved knows what to expect. And, yes, I'm prepared to stick to it! If others weren't satisfied either, we could have a sensible discussion to resolve any outstanding issues. Over time, people grew accustomed to me having an opinion and expecting constructive feedback, which helped build trust among our team members. Still, I believe setting ground rules helps prevent misunderstandings – such as setting priorities clearly before meetings and defining deadlines for each task. In essence, advocating myself isn't necessarily about shouting out loudly; rather, it's finding balance between being

Well, I think it's quite simple really. When I feel like I'm being misunderstood, I try to take a step back and gather my thoughts before responding. Sometimes, talking it out with someone who knows me fairly well helps clarify things. My son is always been very good at getting his perspective across, even when we're having a disagreement. But if all else fails, I suppose I'd ask myself why I felt the way I did initially and what might have led others to misinterpret me. Patience and reflection usually sort everything out in the end.

I love being surrounded by my loved ones, even though we're all scattered a bit now since our son moved away. When we have family gatherings, I usually stick to what feels most enjoyable – chatting with everyone, catching up on each other's lives, sharing stories, and reminiscing about old times. The kids always make sure there's plenty of food to go round, so I like to indulge in some traditional dishes and treats while keeping an eye on those grandkids, making sure they're having fun too! It's lovely seeing them grow and hear about their latest adventures. Occasionally, someone will suggest playing games or watching something together, which is great way to break the ice and create memories. All in all, these family gatherings bring us closer together despite being apart; it warms my heart knowing we still share such strong bonds.

To be honest, I'm not too keen on these modern networking platforms like LinkedIn. As a retiree, my main focus is on staying connected with family and close friends, rather than building an extensive professional network. I mean, why bother when we had face-to-face interactions during workdays, didn't we? But, since some grandkids seem quite taken with them, I suppose I have created a profile there – mostly so I could connect with my son and daughter-in-law, and perhaps make contact with people who share similar interests in local charity events or volunteer opportunities. Really though, it all seems a bit overwhelming and noisy to me; I'd much prefer a simple, handwritten letter any day!

I'd say I'm quite direct and honest, but still considerate of people's feelings. If someone asks me a personal question or shares something vulnerable, I try my best to listen attentively, offer what support I can, and respond truthfully while being empathetic. I believe saying how I feel or think helps build trust and deeper connections. Of course, there are some subjects where tact is more necessary – like discussing finances or health issues – so I'll phrase those conversations carefully to avoid causing unnecessary distress. But overall, I prefer open and genuine dialogue rather than beating around the bush.

Well, I'd say I'm a bit set in my ways, even now in retirement! But I suppose that's what comes from being a strong-willed person who knows her mind. If someone tries to influence me into something I'm not keen on, I'll politely listen to their perspective, but ultimately, I make sure I know why I feel strongly about an issue before making any decisions. It's hard for others to persuade me once I've made up my mind, unless there's compelling evidence or logic backing them out – which isn't always easy to find! I think people sometimes underestimate how stubborn we older folk can be because we're more likely to have had our minds shaped by our youth, and perhaps some lessons were missed along the way. Still, I value respect and open dialogue; I believe that having differing opinions is part of building meaningful relationships. So while I might stand firm on certain issues, I remain receptive to reason and willing to compromise when circumstances warrant it.

I'm quite wary of those targeted ads, to be honest. It feels like a bit too much information being collected about me, even though I know it's all supposed to make them more relevant. Still, I suppose some is better than none. If I really need something, I might tolerate looking at an ad, but I'd rather people didn't track my browsing habits unless absolutely necessary.

To be honest, I struggle a bit with keeping track of all those open windows! My grandsons always laugh at me because I tend to have too many tabs open – usually something to do with emails, Facebook, and maybe some BBC News. It gets quite cluttered after a while. Sometimes I ask them to close unnecessary ones so we can tidy everything up together. They're very helpful like that. Occasionally, I'll even put my computer to sleep overnight and start again fresh the next morning – clears my mind, helps me feel more focused!

Well, I try to rise above them, really. I think one should be kind and courteous even when faced with unpleasantness. It's just a waste of time getting worked up about people who say hurtful things. My late husband always used to tell me to ignore them, and he was quite wise, so I take his advice most of the time. Of course, there have been occasions where I felt like responding, but my granddaughter says I'm very brave to let it go. Anyway, I'd rather focus on spreading positivity and kindness wherever I can. After all, I believe we're only human, and everyone has our struggles – maybe someone out there is having a terrible day! A bit of empathy goes a long way, don't you think?

I usually ask my grandchild, who lives nearby, to give me a hand. He's very handy with computers and helps sort out any issues. Sometimes he'll even record us so we can watch again later if needed. It makes me feel much more comfortable knowing someone is there to assist me while still being able to rely on myself whenever possible.

I stick to a simple system where I make out a list every Thursday morning, prioritising essential payments like utility bills, council tax, and the mortgage. Then I pay those first thing Monday morning. The rest follow suit throughout the month. My bank gives me reminders via post and occasionally sends texts so I'm kept informed. It helps having been brought up in an era where being punctual was valued; now it feels more relaxed, which suits me fine!

I think it's always a bit of an agonising process, to be honest! But, once I take stock of what we're using each service for, and how much money is being spent, I try to weigh up whether it's worth keeping or not. For instance, some streaming services my grandkids have subscribed me to aren't really used enough, so those go first. And any magazine subscriptions that haven't been read for ages probably get axed too. It's all about prioritisation, isn't it? But one thing that does make it easier is having someone like Jo, my granddaughter, who loves technology and tech-savvy, to come round and sort out our devices for us. Sometimes her fresh eyes spot something I wouldn't notice – a dodgy app here, unnecessary upgrade there... saves me wasting more money on something I didn't need! Still, I must admit, even with guidance, cancelling subscriptions still feels like a bit of an ordeal – getting into all the terms and conditions again, making sure everything gets cancelled properly... So perhaps one day I'll figure out how to manage these sorts of decisions better!

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned one when it comes to making major buying decisions. Before we even think about parting with our hard-earned money, I like to sit down with my grandkids, maybe have a cup of tea together, and discuss what we really need rather than just wanting something because everyone else has it. It helps me make sure it'll be put to good use and won't end up gathering dust somewhere. If it's essential – say, replacing a worn-out boiler or upgrading the washing machine – I'd probably go straight to the local market or compare prices between different brands and retailers before making a decision. But for bigger ticket items, I tend to stick to trusted sources, perhaps someone who knows me and understands my values. And let's not forget, there's usually some wiggle room left in the budget, so it's always worth waiting for a sale! My late husband used to tease me about being very frugal; he said I was worse than pinching pennies in a piggy bank! Still, I believe his words hold true – waste not, want not is still a motto I live by.

I'm a thrifty soul, always looking to make sure my money goes as far as it can. When deciding whether to buy something new or second-hand, I think about what will last me longest and give the best value for my hard-earned pennies. If someone is offering a decent deal on a nearly-new item that fits our current lifestyle perfectly, like we need a replacement vacuum cleaner or a new pair of shoes, I'll usually go with the used one. But if it's something essential, like a kitchen appliance or a bed, I'd opt for brand new – safety first! And of course, there's always the option of DIY repairs whenever possible; nothing beats fixing your own stuff instead of throwing away resources. It all comes down to weighing costs against how much hassle and making an informed decision based on priorities.

I'm very much a "waste-not, want-not" kind of person, and avoiding debt has always been a way of life for me since my childhood. You know, growing up during wartime rationing taught us all to be mindful of our spending and make every penny count. Even now, I stick to my rule of thumb – only take out money when absolutely necessary, like replacing worn-out items or paying bills on time. If something isn't essential, I try to find alternatives or wait until I have some savings put aside. My husband was quite strict too; we had a joint account where he'd transfer what little extra income into there each month so we could save together. We didn't go out to eat frequently, instead opting for homemade meals which were affordable and delicious. When I lost him, the discipline really helped me cope financially. It gave me a sense of security knowing I wasn't burdening myself or anyone else with unnecessary expenses. It's simply common sense, I suppose. Live below your means and plan ahead; it makes managing finances so much easier in the end. Now, of course, I still indulge occasionally – maybe buy a nice cup of tea or treat myself to a weekend outing – but those rare indulgences come straight from my savings

I always try to take advantage of seasonal sales whenever I can! You know me, I'm a bit of a thrifter at heart, and I love finding bargains on essential items like cleaning supplies, cookware, and bedding during Black Friday or Boxing Day sales. My grandkids think I'm crazy, but I say why pay full price when you can snag something great at half the cost! That being said, my husband was quite the bargain hunter too, so we'd often work together to find deals on bigger-ticket items like furniture or electronics. Now that he's gone, I still enjoy browsing through catalogues and websites to spot discounts, even though I might end up buying more than I need – an old habit dies hard, I suppose! Anyway, I consider myself lucky to be able to save some money, knowing it'll put towards those lovely garden projects or spoiling the kids/grandkids rotten.

I think a big part of my approach is being mindful of what's available at each mealtime, rather than looking at specific portions. You know, we didn't have all these fancy food labels when I was growing up, so we just ate until we were satisfied, really. Now, I try to listen to my body, stop eating once I feel like I'm full, before getting too uncomfortable. And I find that having smaller meals throughout the day helps me avoid overeating altogether. Plus, cooking for myself now allows me to make sure everything goes into moderation – baked potatoes aren't always huge, and vegetable portions are reasonable! It's simple, yet effective, and suits an older chap like me who wants to take care of himself.

I think a gentle, solution-focused approach is best for me. You know, someone who will listen attentively, ask open-ended questions, and help me work out my thoughts and feelings rather than simply telling me what to do. It's like having a friendly chat, isn't it? If we were going round in circles discussing symptoms all day, I'd lose interest. But if someone could help me identify patterns and find practical solutions, that would be much more helpful. Plus, I'm not one for dwelling on past regrets – so I need someone who can steer us towards moving forward, gently guiding me where needed. And please, make sure there's plenty of tea available!

Preventing burnout is a big one for me, isn't it? You know, people always say "take breaks" and all that, but for someone like me who used to be so driven by work and responsibilities, finding balance has been key. For me, setting aside dedicated blocks of time for myself each day – usually between 1 pm and 2 pm, before the post-lunch slump hits – allows me to recharge. It might sound simple, but taking short strolls outside or curling up with a good book really helps to refresh my mind. And of course, having something enjoyable to look forward to later in the day gives me motivation throughout those quieter moments. Plus, keeping my priorities straight and focusing on what's truly important hasn't helped too much; knowing exactly where my spending money goes and sticking to a budget helps alleviate some stress, and being realistic about how many tasks I should realistically complete each day prevents feeling overwhelmed.

I think my main thing is sticking to a regular bedtime routine, which helps me wind down and prepares me for bed. It starts with getting ready a bit earlier, having a warm bath, putting on some soothing music, and maybe even practicing some gentle stretches before settling into bed. And of course, keeping an eye on what I eat during the day isn't too bad either – avoiding heavy meals close to bedtime seems to make all the difference. Plus, making sure my room is dark, quiet, and cool really does help. I find that consistency is key; once I establish those habits, I tend to have better restful nights.

I'm quite keen on keeping fit, despite being a bit older now. My exercise routine has evolved slightly since my retirement, though. In the morning, I like to start with some gentle stretching exercises while having a cup of tea – helps me warm up properly for the rest of the day. Then, I usually take a brisk walk round the block, which gets my heart rate up nicely. It's lovely to breathe in some fresh air and watch the world waking up. In the afternoons, I might pop into the local park for a more leisurely stroll. The scenery is always changing with the seasons, so there's something different every month. Sometimes I'll join forces with one of our neighbourhood ladies who shares an interest in gardening; we chat while tending to our plants – nice way to pass the time. Occasionally, I treat myself to a yoga session at the community centre nearby. Not too intense, mind – I prefer something calming rather than challenging. A bit of flexibility never hurts, eh!

I'd say I'm a very logical thinker, always try to weigh up the pros and cons before making a decision. It's much easier said than done, isn't it? But really, it comes down to considering what will work best for me and my situation. Sometimes, you have to trust your instincts too – if something feels right, even if there might be some doubts lurking beneath the surface. For instance, when deciding which vegetables to plant in our little garden, I like to think about what'll thrive in our climate and soil type, and how easy they're going to be to harvest. Then, I look into any specific advice from experts or other gardeners who share similar conditions. And finally, I take note of what my husband used to tell me – he was quite handy with plants! Of course, we didn't agree on everything, but his input usually made sense. In more serious decisions, though, I rely heavily on logic and facts rather than intuition. When choosing between job opportunities, for example, I consider factors such as salary, benefits, security, growth prospects...all those sorts of things. Research is key; sometimes, all the information available seems overwhelming, so I jot notes and revisit them until I feel informed enough to make an educated choice

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned soul, so I suppose I tend to rely more on common sense and practical advice rather than fancy management buzzwords. When someone gives me feedback, I try to listen attentively and think about what they're saying. If there's something specific they'd like me to improve upon, I'll make a note of it and ask clarifying questions if needed. Then, I try to consider all sides of the issue – my own perspective, theirs, and any relevant facts or data. It might take some time to weigh everything out, but once I have a better grasp of the situation, I start brainstorming potential solutions. Sometimes these involve making changes to procedures, other times it involves having a chat with colleagues or superiors to explain how we could tackle the issue together. What really helps is getting people involved who are invested in finding a solution that works. Whether it was during my teaching days or now as a retiree, I believe collective input leads to better outcomes. As long as everyone is open to constructive discussion and willing to put thought into solving our shared problems, I find we come up with workable plans. And I always appreciate when others share their expertise too!

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite methodical. You know, a bit like taking a step-by-step route rather than jumping straight into something. When faced with a puzzle or decision-making task, I try to gather all relevant information first – what facts, figures, and insights can be found. Then, I take some time to sort them out logically, weighing one against another, considering different angles and possibilities. It's almost like creating an mental map of sorts. Once I have everything laid bare, I look for patterns and connections between these pieces of data. Sometimes it helps to pause and review my findings before making any conclusions. And, of course, being able to explain my reasoning clearly to someone else always seems to clarify my thoughts too! After all, I believe getting others involved in the process can offer fresh eyes and valuable feedback. Ultimately, my aim is to find a solution that balances logic and common sense – nothing too radical, but sensible and effective. As my grandmother used to say, "Patience and curiosity will serve you better."

I find that taking a step back, making notes, and rephrasing what someone has said helps me grasp the underlying idea better. Sometimes, asking myself "what does he/she mean exactly?" or "how does that apply to my situation" really gets me into focusing mode. Writing things down too can be helpful; there's something about seeing words on paper that makes sense of them for me. And yes, explaining it out loud is always a great way – talking through an issue usually clarifies any misunderstandings. A cuppa tea never hurts either!

I think one way I try to identify areas for growth is by reflecting on what's been bothering me lately. For instance, I recently found myself getting frustrated while using my tablet because I'm still struggling a bit with updating everything and figuring out all these new features. My grandchild was really helpful in explaining some things to me, so I realized maybe I should ask them more often instead of getting stuck. Another area where I'd like to improve is staying up-to-date with digital security practices – I know I need to be more cautious, but sometimes those emails and messages can look very similar, making it hard to tell which ones are safe and which aren't! It makes me nervous, to be honest. It might sound silly, but being able to figure out how to troubleshoot issues on my devices has become an ongoing battle. If someone could give me step-by-step instructions, perhaps I'll feel more empowered to tackle those tricky technical bits. A bit of confidence boost wouldn't go amiss either! What do you make of it? Am I too picky?

Well, you know, I think I'm a bit like my grandmother used to say: "Take one step at a time." For me, approaching something new is all about breaking it down into manageable bits. It helps to focus on what I need to achieve, rather than feeling overwhelmed by everything else. And I always try to remind myself why I wanted to take on this new situation in the first place – whether it's meeting someone new, trying out a hobby, or even taking an online course. Having a sense of purpose makes it feel more worthwhile. Plus, I find that having a trusted friend or confidant nearby can make all the difference; having someone who knows me well enough to offer support and guidance really helps to alleviate some of the uncertainty. Of course, there will be moments where I'll still struggle, but that's okay! As long as I remember that it's normal to stumble sometimes, I should be able to bounce back relatively unscathed. After all, I've learned that patience and curiosity get you much further than rushing through life ever did...

I always make sure to prioritize my love of reading whenever I can. Even though I'm fully retired now, I still try to fit in a bit of reading each morning before starting my daily routine. There's something so comforting about curling up with a cuppa and getting lost in a good story or learning something new. My granddaughter is actually really keen to spend time together too, which makes me feel more motivated to squeeze in some reading during her visit. We might have a nice chat while I bake a cake or work on our jigsaw puzzle together – those moments are lovely! It's all about finding little pockets of time that suit us, isn't it?

I'm rather particular about what I read, so I tend to stick to a few favourite authors and genres. However, my grandkids have been great at introducing me to some wonderful books lately. They're always eager to share something interesting they've found and love getting recommendations from them. For example, they introduced me to Philippa Gregory last summer – her historical novels are fantastic! And more recently, we stumbled upon Anne Tyler together; her writing is really thought-provoking and engaging. But generally, I find most enjoyable writers who tell strong, character-driven stories set against an intriguing backdrop of history, though sometimes even those can be quite different depending on how one interprets the past. If someone recommends an author or series based on our conversation or shared tastes, I appreciate the suggestion greatly.

I love listening to music while engaging in various activities. When I'm feeling energetic, I might put on some lively tunes and tidy my living room, putting away books or tidying the furniture. It really helps me get into a rhythm! If I'm cooking something for the grandkids, I'll typically listen to more upbeat music – perhaps some traditional folk or classic pop. The nostalgia always makes them smile, and we have such lovely conversations afterwards. Sometimes, though, I find myself lost in thought while listening to instrumental pieces; I appreciate how calming it is to let my mind wander like that.

I rather like a mix of both, actually. There's something comforting about being able to listen while still having my hands free, isn't there? But, I have to admit, sometimes I find myself getting distracted and missing out on what's being said because I'm too busy tidying up the kitchen or sorting the laundry. Still, I think audiobooks can be lovely, especially for longer journeys or during chores. I've got an awful lot of them lined up to finish now – lots of Agatha Christie mysteries and some history books about the local area where we used to live before moving here.

I love staying active! Since retiring, I try to make time for my hobbies every chance I have. Gardening is a big one – there's something so satisfying about growing your own vegetables and watching them flourish. My small garden may be quite tiny, but it brings me joy all season round. In addition to tending to my plants, I also really enjoy taking part in local volunteer work. We go along to our village hall where we tidy up, serve refreshments, or even host little exhibitions like flower shows. It's lovely being among people who share similar interests and getting some exercise while chatting together too! Occasionally, I'll take a walk with some of the lads from my bridge club; those ones always keep me going, laughing away until we reach tea time anyway! Sometimes, though, an afternoon stroll into town sounds more appealing. The fresh air does wonders for clearing out any cobwebs, and browsing in the shops never hurts either...

I must say, I rather cherish my alone time. There's something so comforting about having a moment to myself where I can sit quietly, perhaps with a cuppa and a good book, and let my mind wander. It helps me recharge and reflect on all the lovely moments spent with loved ones, like my grandkids. Sometimes, I'll take a walk round the block, enjoying the fresh air and watching people go about their daily lives. I find it quite peaceful, really. It gives me a chance to be still and listen to my thoughts – I've found that patience and curiosity have gotten me far in life!

When trying a new cuisine, I like to start with something simple, really. Maybe some Indian food, like chicken tikka masala or vegetable korma – those sorts of dishes are usually quite forgiving if you're not entirely sure what you're getting into. And, of course, I always try to stick to traditional recipes whenever possible; there's something comforting about knowing exactly how it should taste. I find that once you get past the initial hesitation, people generally appreciate your willingness to try new foods. My grandkids love teaching me different cooking techniques, too – we'll spend an hour making naan bread together, and before you know it, we're having a lovely meal! It's all about being open-minded and enjoying the process, isn't it? Still, I must admit, sometimes I prefer sticking to my tried-and-tested favourite dishes – a hearty roast dinner or a warm slice of apple pie is hard to beat! But, yes, exploring new flavours has become more appealing since retiring and having more time to experiment in the kitchen myself.

I think what helps me mix things up is my love of gardening and cooking. There's something therapeutic about getting out into nature and watching plants grow, isn't there? And I find experimenting with different vegetables and herbs really enjoyable. But I'm not just a one-trick pony – I like having some other hobbies too. For instance, I have a regular book club where we meet once a month to discuss whatever novel we're currently reading, which always leads to interesting conversations. Sometimes, I'll take advantage of our local community centre offering classes in areas I wouldn't normally try myself, so I might attend a painting session or join in with a language exchange programme – those sorts of tings give me a chance to meet people and broaden my horizons. Occasionally, I'll treat myself to an event outside the usual, such as a concert or theatre performance, because it gets us all excited to plan ahead and look forward to it. What works best is finding little pockets of activity throughout the week rather than cramming everything into one go; it prevents feeling burnt out!

I'm a bit old-fashioned, I suppose! For my main hobby, gardening, I have an old fashioned notebook where I jot down notes on what I planted, how much water I gave them, any pests we had issues with... all that sort of thing. It helps me remember which plants need more watering or pruning. My grandkids find it amusing because I still type out little reminders on paper – they're used to digital everything now! For keeping track of our local events and volunteer work, I rely heavily on my planner calendar. Simple, effective, and easy to read even for someone like me who isn't exactly tech-savvy! Occasionally, I'll take some photographs of new plants and flowers, too, so I can refer to those later. It might sound old-fashioned, but there's something satisfying about putting pen to paper and writing things down. Don't know why, maybe it's just habit, but it feels rather therapeutic, actually. Now, give me a cuppa and let's chat about your favourite way of documenting leisure activities!

I'm quite particular about keeping my belongings tidy, which helps me feel more organised overall. For example, I have a designated spot for everything – keys, wallet, bags, and whatnot. My grandkids always say I must be a stickler because I colour-code our notes and reminders so we know who wrote them! As for digital organisation, I have an email folder system where I categorise messages into different groups like personal, bills, work-related stuff... It might sound simple, but it really does make all the difference! My late husband used to tease me about being too fussy, but he'd find his way to whatever I needed him to look for, even if it took him ages! In fact, I still have my trusty A5 notebook where I jot down quick reminders, birthdays, anniversaries, doctor's appointments... You name it. The kids found out how much I rely on these little scribbles during the summer holidays; now they give me mine every morning before they go off to school! It sounds silly perhaps, but having something tangible makes sense to me. Nowadays, I also use sticky notes and calendars to plan ahead, though sometimes I wish there were fewer reminders since my brain gets a bit clogged up! Still,

Before leaving my home, I make a cuppa and have a quick tidy of the living room while getting ready. It usually takes me about an hour to be fully prepared. I check the weather forecast so I know what clothes to wear, and plan out my route using the bus timetable to avoid any disruptions. I pack whatever essentials like sandwiches, water, and books into a bag, which is quite simple really. Occasionally, I might stop to visit the local post office or bank to collect mail or deposit cheques, all very ordinary routines. Sometimes, if there's something specific I need to pick up from the shop or library, I'll take a bit more time. After finishing getting ready, I'm generally set to go!

I think one should start with what we have, rather than feeling like we need a lot of fancy gadgets to save energy. For instance, turning off lights and electronics when not needed is simple enough, isn't it? And keeping curtains closed during winter helps retain heat. We've got double glazing already, which has made a big difference. My grandkids say our little cottage feels warm even in winter now. I try to be mindful of my daily routines – taking shorter showers, using the kettle instead of boiling water three times because I'm making tea, all those sorts of tiny changes add up. It might sound silly, but every bit counts! Also, planting some more plants outside will help with insulation too; nature does its part to regulate temperature quite effectively. Simple habits really make a noticeable impact over time, don't you agree?

I must admit, I'm still getting used to them all! My grandsons have been setting me up with these new-fangled contraptions like Alexa and Google Home. They're very helpful, really, but sometimes I feel a bit bewildered by all the options and settings. To be honest, I'd much rather stick with my trusty radio and television – there's something comforting about having control over what I watch and listen to. That being said, I suppose they do make some things easier, like controlling the heating and lighting, which is quite convenient during those cold winter months. But overall, I think I'll stick to using them primarily for simple tasks and save more complicated ones for someone who understands how they work better... perhaps one of my tech-savvy grandkids!

I think prioritising competing goals is a bit like tending to my garden – you have to assess what's urgent and what's essential. For me, staying healthy and independent takes top priority; I'd hate to compromise my mobility too much now that I'm getting older. So, I make sure to take regular breaks throughout the day to rest those joints. Family time is another big one – spending quality moments with my grandkids makes all the effort worthwhile. If there's something specific that comes along, like fixing the washing machine, I'll tackle that straight away because it affects daily routines. But overall, I try to be mindful of what really matters and allocate resources accordingly. My late husband always said "waste not, want not," so I believe in making every penny count while still allowing myself some comfort. Of course, setting aside money for future expenses and being prepared for any eventualities helps give me peace of mind. It's all about striking a balance between the present and the future.
